perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges that its breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable damage to Disclosing Party and hereby agrees that, in addition to any other relief available at law or in equity, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that its breach of this Agreement may cause damage to Disclosing Party. The Disclosing Party may seek relief under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'irreparable' and specifying 'injunctive relief', we remove consumer protections in place when determining financial compensation. This may impede fair compensation, should the consumer feel the compensation is inadequate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Legal Remedies Act - Cal. Civ. Code § 1770
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the remedies available to the Disclosing Party (consumer) in case of a breach, potentially conflicting with the broad remedial scope intended by the CLRA, which aims to protect consumers against deceptive practices by ensuring adequate compensation.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person d
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific remedy of 'injunctive relief' and the qualifier 'irreparable' related to the damage caused by a breach, replacing it with a more general statement about seeking 'relief'. The Law Explanation states this weakens the remedies available to the Disclosing Party, potentially conflicting with the CLRA which aims to protect consumers by ensuring adequate compensation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 1770(a), indicates that unfair or deceptive acts or practices in transactions are unlawful. By removing 'irreparable damage' and 'injunctive relief', the contract may limit the consumer's ability to seek full compensation, potentially constituting an unfair practice as it weakens the consumer's position in case of a breach. This constitutes an Omission Legal Contradiction, as legally mandated consumer protection is omitted.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any action related thereto will be governed, controlled, interpreted, and defined by and under the laws of the State of California , without giving effect to any conflicts of laws principles that require the application of the law of a different state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any action related thereto will be governed, controlled, interpreted, and defined by and under the laws determined by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Disclosing Party (Evelozcity) to choose the governing law, which could include jurisdictions with less consumer protection, violating California's interest in protecting its residents and undermining established conflict-of-laws principles. The Recipient cannot challenge this decision.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification undermines the principle that California courts should assert jurisdiction to protect the rights of California residents, particularly consumers, which directly contradicts the state's established legal framework.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.10. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the Disclosing Party to choose the governing law, potentially circumventing California law, which undermines the principle that California courts may assert jurisdiction to protect the rights of California residents. CCP 410.10 states that a California court may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or the United States. By allowing the Disclosing Party to select the governing law, the contract introduces the possibility that a different jurisdiction's laws could be applied, potentially in conflict with California's interest in protecting its residents, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended or modified only by written instrument executed by both parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended or modified by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for mutual agreement, the Disclosing Party can unilaterally change the contract terms, potentially removing or altering consumer rights without the Recipient's consent. This violates California contract law principles requiring mutual assent for contract modifications.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts California Civil Code § 1698, which requires that a contract in writing may be altered or modified by a contract in writing, or by an executed oral agreement, and if not in writing, requires new consideration, which both require the agreement of all parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1698 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. EXTINCTION OF CONTRACTS [1682 - 1701] ( Title 5 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Modification and Cancellation [1697 - 1701] ( Heading of Chapter 3 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109. ) 1698. (a) A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing. (b) A contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement to the extent that the oral agreement is executed by the parties. (c) Unless the contract otherwise expressly provides, a contract in writing may be modified by an oral agreement supported by new consideration. The statute of frauds (Section 1624) is required to be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (d) Nothing in this section precludes in an appropriate case the application of rules of law concerning estoppel, oral novation and substitution of a new agreement, rescission of a written contract by an oral agreement, waiver of a provision of a written contract, or oral independent collateral contracts. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1976, Ch. 109.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1698, which governs how written contracts can be modified. The original text required mutual agreement for amendments, ensuring both parties consented to changes. The changed text allows the Disclosing Party to unilaterally modify the agreement, removing the Recipient's right to consent. Snippet 1 (CIV 1698) states, 'A contract in writing may be modified by a contract in writing' and describes further conditions requiring agreement, which are violated when one party can unilaterally modify the contract. The omission of the requirement for mutual agreement creates a clear contradiction, as it permits modifications without the consent of all parties, violating fundamental contract law principles.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations c ontained in this Agreement expire and are of no further force or effect five (5) years from last date that Confidential Information was disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations contained in this Agreement never expire.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the time limit on confidentiality, potentially preventing the Recipient from using information that could become public knowledge, thereby restricting their ability to compete or innovate freely after a reasonable period. This could violate California's policy against restraints of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially restrains the Recipient's ability to engage in their profession or trade by indefinitely restricting their use of information, thereby contravening California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally prohibits restraints of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by removing the time limit on confidentiality obligations, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code § 16600. The original text limited the confidentiality obligations to five years. The changed text states that the obligations 'never expire.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By indefinitely restricting the use of information, the modified contract could restrain the recipient's ability to engage in their trade, contradicting this law.
